actions.thought: On the button in a 3-handed game, I have a wide opening range. While 7s6c is a speculative offsuit connector, it is worth a standard raise to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot with positional advantage. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind).

I am dealt 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Being in the small blind, I am OOP (out of position) for the remainder of the hand against the Button. There is no strategic value in calling or raising with this holding, so I will fold.

With Pocket Jacks in the Big Blind facing a standard Button open, we have a very strong hand that dominates a large portion of the Button's opening range. A 3-bet here is mandatory for value and to deny equity from overcards. I will size up to 1100 (nearly 4x his raise) due to being out of position, which will put maximum pressure on medium pairs and Broadway combinations while building the pot with a premium holding.

I opened from the button with 7s6c, a speculative hand. Pro in the big blind responded with a significant 3-bet to 1100. My hand is at the very bottom of my opening range and does not play well enough post-flop to justify calling a 3.7x 3-bet out of position, even though I have position for the rest of the hand. The pot odds (requiring ~35% equity) are not favorable given the strength of a typical 3-bet range here. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

I am on the button with Td8d, a suited connector that plays well in position. The blinds are 50/100 and no one has entered the pot yet. Opening to 300 is a standard raise size to steal the blinds and take the initiative in the hand, allowing me to control the action.
We are in the small blind with 98 offsuit facing a button raise. This is a weak, unsuited hand that plays poorly out of position. Folding is the most standard and profitable long-term decision.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 2d. Flashlight raised to 3x from the button. While these cards are suited and connected, they are the lowest possible rank and I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling 200 more into a 450 pot gives me decent odds, but the low equity and poor playability of 32s out of position makes this a standard fold to a 3x open early in the tournament.

Playing 3-handed, K4s is a reasonable hand to open from the button. We have positional advantage, card removal with the King, and flush potential. A standard raise to 2.5x BB (250) applies pressure on the blinds and gives us a good chance to win the pot uncontested or play a heads-up pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 4h. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x. While the hand is suited, 84s is too weak to profitably defend out of position against a button open in a three-handed game, especially against a player who has shown aggression. Calling puts me in a difficult spot post-flop without the lead, and 3-betting as a bluff is risky given the hand's limited playability if called. The most disciplined play here is to fold.

I am in the big blind with 3-2 offsuit, which is among the weakest possible starting hands. Although the pot odds are tempting, calling out of position with such a disconnected, low-equity hand against a button raise is negative expected value. Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8h 6c. While it is tempting to open a wide range in a 3-handed game, 8h 6c is quite weak and doesn't play well post-flop. Given that Pro (Big Blind) has already shown aggression by 3-betting in Hand #1, I will choose to play conservatively and fold this hand to avoid getting into a difficult spot out of position or being forced to fold to a 3-bet.

I am in the small blind with King-Four suited. The button has folded, making this a heads-up pot against the big blind. While K4s is a reasonable hand, it is relatively weak to play aggressively pre-flop. Completing the blind allows me to see a flop cheaply and evaluate the board. Therefore, I will call the additional 50 chips to match the big blind.

Flashlight just limped from the Small Blind. I have A2 suited in the Big Blind. While raising to isolate and punish the weak limp is an option, checking is also perfectly fine to see a free flop in position with a speculative hand that has great flush potential but weak high-card value. I'll check to realize equity without unnecessarily bloating the pot pre-flop.

The flop is As 7s 9d. I hold Kh 4h, which missed entirely. Since the pre-flop action was checked through, neither player has shown significant strength. Betting here with only King-high into an Ace-high board is risky and unlikely to fold out many hands that have connected with the board. Checking is the best move to maintain pot control and see a free card, while preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a board with several draws (spades, straight potential). Flashlight limped pre-flop and checked the flop, which often indicates marginal showdown value or a draw rather than a monster. I will bet half-pot to extract value from draws, middle pairs, or worse Aces, while simultaneously protecting my hand by denying free cards.

The flop is As 7s 9d and I hold Kh 4h. I have no pair and no real draw, essentially holding only King-high. My opponent, Pro, has bet 100 into a 100 pot, which indicates they likely have an Ace or are representing one on this dry board. There is no justification to call with such a weak hand out of position. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to minimize losses.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game to attempt a steal from the blinds. Since there has been no action yet, opening the pot with a raise is the standard aggressive play to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or play the hand in position.
I hold Qs 3h in the small blind, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a 2.5x open raise from the button, the optimal play is to simply fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. The button (Flashlight) opened for 250, and the Small Blind folded. A8o is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a standard button open in a 3-handed game. I have decent pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 400) and A8o has good equity against a typical button range. Calling allows me to see a flop without bloating the pot out of position.

On a paired board of Kc-5s-Ks, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight). My Ace-high has some showdown value, but the board heavily favors the range of the button raiser, who is likely to hold many Kings or pocket pairs. Donk betting into this board is generally ill-advised. Checking is the standard play to see how the button responds. I will check and evaluate based on the size of their continuation bet, if they make one.

The board is paired with Kings, which is a favorable texture for my pre-flop opening range. I currently hold Jack-high with no showdown value, but I maintain the initiative. A continuation bet is standard here to represent strength and put pressure on the opponent, who likely missed this board or holds a weak pocket pair. I will bet approximately one-third of the pot to minimize risk while maintaining aggression.

The board is Kc-5s-Ks, and I am holding Ah-8c. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has made a small continuation bet of 180 into a 550 pot (about 33% pot size). My Ace-high has significant showdown value on this dry, paired board, especially against a button opening range that contains many air hands and missed broadways. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 180 to win a total pot of 910) to see a turn. Folding is too weak against a standard c-bet, and raising would likely only get called by better hands (Kings or pocket pairs). I will call and evaluate the turn.

The turn 9h is essentially a blank card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I am holding Ace-high (A8o), which retains some showdown value against the button's opening range. Since I am out of position and Flashlight was the aggressor on the previous streets, checking is the most standard and prudent play. It allows me to see how the button responds—if they check back, I likely have the best hand; if they bet again, I will have to evaluate the size of the bet relative to the strength of my Ace-high and the likelihood of them continuing a bluff on this paired board.

The board remains paired with Kings, a texture that is difficult to bluff on against a calling range, and the turn nine does not improve my Jack-high hand. Since the opponent checked, betting again would likely only be called by better hands (like a King, a pair of nines, or a pair of fives). Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, preserve my stack, and see a free river card, which is the most disciplined and strategic play in this position.

On the river 2d, the board is Kc-5s-Ks-9h-2d. I am holding Ace-high (Ah-8c). The turn went check-check, which suggests that Flashlight (the button) likely does not have a monster hand like a King or a full house, as those hands usually continue betting for value. Flashlight's range now likely consists of middle-to-low pocket pairs, other Ace-high hands, or total air. My Ace-high has significant showdown value in this line. Betting doesn't achieve much because I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I probably won't fold out many better hands like pocket pairs. By checking, I can either win the pot at showdown if Flashlight checks back or decide whether to call a bluff if Flashlight bets. Given the action, checking is the most profitable and safest play.

My opponent checked the river on a board of Kc-5s-Ks-9h-2d. I hold Jack-high (Jh-Tc). My opponent has shown passivity by checking the turn and the river. Betting now would be a pure bluff, and since my opponent is likely to call with any pair (including small pairs, fives, nines, or deuces), a bluff is unlikely to be profitable. Checking back allows me to reach showdown with my current hand, which is the most disciplined and strategic play to minimize potential losses when I have no showdown value.

4d 7d is a weak suited two-gapper. Even on the button in 3-handed play, it's generally a fold, as it lacks high card value and playability against 3-bets or post-flop aggression. Folding is the standard and safest play.

In a blind-vs-blind situation with 8h 6h, I have a decent speculative hand that benefits from initiative. Raising to 300 is a standard open to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Holding A-10 offsuit in the big blind against a small blind opening raise, I have a clear advantage. 3-betting is the optimal move here to exert pressure on the small blind's wider range, take control of the initiative, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Raising to 900 is a standard, effective size to put the opponent to a difficult decision.

I opened with a speculative hand (8h 6h) from the Small Blind to take down the blinds or play a post-flop pot with initiative. However, Flashlight's 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind puts me in a difficult position. Calling 600 more to play a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand like 8-high suited is generally -EV (negative expected value). While the hand has some straight and flush potential, the disadvantage of being first to act on all subsequent streets makes it a fold against a standard 3-bet range. I will preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous spot.

I am on the Button with Jack-Ten offsuit (Jh Tc), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I have a stack of nearly 100 big blinds. My standard strategy from the button is to open-raise to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a consistent sizing based on my previous actions and allows me to play the hand in position if either opponent calls.

K6o is a weak hand to defend out of position against a button raise. Defending from the small blind with this holding against a 3x open is negative expected value, especially with the big blind still to act behind me. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve stack equity.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Big Blind. A9o is a decent hand against a likely wide Button opening range. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 450), I have enough equity to defend my blind. I will flat call to see a flop, keeping the pot manageable while playing out of position.

The flop is quite low and disconnected, completely missing my Ac9d. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them. I will see what Flash does before deciding if A-high has any showdown value or if I should just release my hand to a bet.

The flop of 8c-4h-3d is a very dry and disconnected board that likely missed the Big Blind's calling range. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is an ideal spot for a continuation bet. I have two overcards (Jack and Ten) and the board texture makes it difficult for the opponent to continue without a piece of the board or a pocket pair. I will bet approximately 40% of the pot to apply pressure and take down the pot immediately.

The flop completely missed my hand and I am out of position. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor on this dry 8-high board, calling with just Ace-nine high is unprofitable. My hand has no strong draws and minimal showdown value. Folding is the safest and optimal choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-Six suited, which is a playable hand for an opening raise to steal the blinds and maintain position. A 2.5x raise is a standard size that allows me to control the pot size and put pressure on the blinds.

Against a Button open, pocket 8s is a strong hand that frequently has the best equity. However, playing out of position from the Small Blind post-flop can be difficult. A 3-bet helps to isolate the Button, denies equity to the Big Blind, and can potentially win the pot pre-flop against a wide Button opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d7h, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened from the button and Pro 3-bet from the small blind. Calling a 3-bet with 47o is mathematically incorrect and will lead to difficult post-flop situations out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I opened King-Six suited on the button, and the Small Blind has 3-bet me. Given my positional advantage, I should not fold a suited king here. The pot odds are reasonable to call, allowing me to see a flop and potentially outplay the opponent or hit a strong draw or pair. A 4-bet is too aggressive with this holding, so calling is the most balanced and strategic play.

The flop of Qc Ks As heavily favors the pre-flop caller's range, which includes many broadway combos like AK, AQ, AJ, KQ, and sets. My underpair of 8s has virtually no equity and no viable draws on this extremely wet and high board. Betting here would be a bluff against a strong range that rarely folds, so checking with the intention of folding to a bet is the only sensible play.

The board texture [Qc, Ks, As] is highly coordinated and dangerous, hitting a significant portion of an opponent's 3-betting range. My hand, a pair of Kings with a weak kicker, is a bluff catcher at best on this board. Since the opponent checked to me, taking a free card by checking behind is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and avoid committing more chips into a potentially trapped situation or a dominated position.

The turn card is a blank. My pocket 8s remain an underpair to the heavily coordinated broadway board (Q-K-A). Since the flop checked through, my opponent might have a weak pair, a draw, or could be trapping, but my hand still has essentially zero value. Checking is the only sensible move, and I am prepared to fold to any bet.

The opponent has checked to me on the turn, suggesting they do not have a strong hand like a set or a premium Ace-high. My two pair (Kings and Sixes) is currently a strong holding that beats most of the opponent's 3-betting range in this spot. Checking behind again would allow for free cards that could easily improve a potential straight or flush draw to beat me. Betting 900 (approximately half the pot) is the optimal way to gain value from weaker pairs or Ace-high hands and provides some protection against draws.

The board is extremely hostile to pocket eights, featuring three broadway cards (A-K-Q). My opponent's pre-flop calling range is heavily saturated with broadway combinations that connect well with this board. I checked the turn with the intention of giving up, and facing a half-pot bet, my hand has virtually zero equity and no reasonable draws. Folding is the only correct play.

Pre-flop on the Button with 6d 9h, an offsuit two-gapper. This hand is very weak and not playable even from the Button, especially with two opponents left to act. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-2 suited. While the suitedness adds a tiny bit of value, 7-2 is notoriously one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. However, Flashlight (BB) has shown some aggression in previous hands (3-betting in Hand 6). Attempting to steal with such a weak hand out of position is risky, and limping often leads to being raised or playing a weak hand out of position post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with suited gappers (4d 6d). In a three-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to either take the blinds immediately or play a pot in position. I've used a 3x open size (300) in previous hands, so I will maintain consistency with that sizing.

Q5 suited is a weak hand to defend from the Small Blind out of position against a button raise. The pot odds do not justify a call, and playing out of position against an active player like Flash is suboptimal. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I have 6c2d offsuit, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a button raise, folding is the only profitable play. We don't want to get involved with this trash hand out of position.

I am in the button position with 8h 2h, which is a very weak hand with minimal playability. Investing chips to call the big blind with such a marginal holding is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am dealt a premium hand, Ace-King offsuit, in the small blind. With the button folding, I have a clear open-raise situation. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard size that builds the pot for value while putting pressure on the big blind to defend with weaker hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 5d. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600 total. 3d 5d is a speculative suited hand that performs reasonably well in head-up pots from the blinds. Given the 2:1 pot odds and the fact that we are early in the tournament with deep stacks, calling to see a flop is a sound decision to potentially crack a stronger starting hand or navigate post-flop.

Missing the J-9-3 rainbow flop out of position with Ace-King offsuit, my hand lacks strong draws but retains decent showdown value. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate Flash's action. This flop connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's defending range (Jx, 9x, straight draws like QT/T8), so checking is the safer, standard play to avoid building a big pot out of position without a made hand.

I have bottom pair (Threes) on a board of Js-9d-3c. My opponent (Pro), who was the pre-flop aggressor from the Small Blind, has checked to me. This check could indicate a marginal hand, a missed broadway draw, or a trap with a strong hand. Given the board texture, I have some showdown value but my hand is vulnerable to many overcards. Checking back allows me to see a turn card for free, keep the pot small with a weak pair, and potentially induce a bluff on the turn or improve to two pair or trips. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call many streets.

The turn is a 6d, and my Ace-King still has not improved. Since the flop checked through, it's possible my AK high is currently the best hand, but betting now is likely only to be called by better hands like pairs or strong draws. Checking for pot control and aiming to see a cheap showdown or evaluating my opponent's action is the most sound decision.

I have bottom pair (3s) and have picked up a diamond flush draw on the turn. My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked both the flop and the turn. This sequence often indicates a weak hand or a desire to reach showdown cheaply. While I could bet to try and take the pot now, my hand has showdown value against their air (like AK/AQ) and significant equity to improve on the river. Checking back allows me to realize that equity for free and keep the pot small with a marginal hand.

The river is a blank 2c. Both players have checked the flop and the turn. My Ace-King high has reasonable showdown value, especially since my opponent has shown no interest in betting. If I bet now, I am only getting called by better hands (like any pair) and folding out worse hands, so a value bet doesn't make sense. Checking to see a free showdown is the best line.

I have bottom pair (threes) on a board of J-9-3-6-2. The pre-flop aggressor has checked on the flop, turn, and river. This passive line suggests they likely have a weak hand or a missed draw like Ace-high. Since my hand has some showdown value against their likely range of missed high cards, and I am unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet, checking back to realize my showdown value and take the pot is the most prudent play.
I am on the button with a low pocket pair (33). This is a strong enough hand to open-raise to try and steal the blinds or play for set value post-flop. A standard 2.5x open (250) is appropriate here.

Q7o is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. In a 3-handed game, while ranges are wider, calling 200 more from the small blind with a hand that doesn't flop well is generally a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the big blind with King-Nine offsuit, which is a standard hand to defend against a button open. Pro has shown aggression throughout the session, but my hand has sufficient value to call and see a flop with favorable pot odds.

I am out of position in the big blind against the pre-flop aggressor. The board is paired (7-8-8) and my King-high is currently nothing but a bluff catcher at best. It is standard practice to check to the aggressor when out of position on this texture to see their continuation frequency. I have no reason to lead out with a bet into a capped range on this paired board.

On a 7-8-8 paired board, our 33 has some marginal showdown value, but we are well behind any 7, 8, or higher pocket pair. Betting here will mostly get called by better hands, and we risk getting check-raised out of our equity. Checking behind for pot control and seeing a free turn is the standard line to realize our equity.

The board is 7d-8c-8h-4c. Both players checked the flop, which suggests neither has a strong hand (like an eight or a pocket pair). My King-nine offsuit remains a weak hand with limited showdown value. Betting here would essentially be turning my hand into a bluff against a range that likely consists of overcards or air, which is not profitable. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and reach the river for free.

Flashlight has checked to us again on the turn. Our pocket threes remain very vulnerable on this board and hold only marginal showdown value. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, such as a pair of sevens, fours, or club flush draws, while folding out hands we are currently beating or flipping against, like high-card air. Checking behind allows us to maintain pot control, avoid getting check-raised out of our meager equity, and safely navigate to the river.

The board is paired with 8s and 4s, and my hand is King-high. Both players checked the flop and turn, which significantly caps the opponent's range, suggesting they likely have missed overcards or a weak pair. Betting into this range is unlikely to fold out better hands and there is little value in bluffing when I have some potential showdown value against other high-card hands. Checking is the standard play to reach the river and realize any potential showdown value.

My pocket 3s have been counterfeited by the 88447 board, meaning my best five-card hand is playing the board. I will lose at showdown to any opponent holding a single card higher than a 7. Since checking guarantees a loss against almost any sensible holding, this is a prime opportunity to turn my hand into a bluff. Flashlight's triple-check indicates a lack of strong showdown value (like an 8 or an overpair) and points towards missed high cards. A bet of 350 chips credibly represents a 7, a 4, or a medium pocket pair, and applies strong pressure on his Ace-high or King-high hands to fold.

The board is 7d-8c-8h-4c-4h, which is a double-paired board. My hand is Kc-9s, which gives me 8-8-4-4-K as my best five-card combination. Because the opponent checked the flop and turn, they are unlikely to hold an Ace, King, or Queen, as an aggressive player would likely have bet those hands earlier for value. My King-high effectively beats any 7-high, 6-high, 5-high, 3-high, or 2-high combinations, and it splits against any other 9-high. Given the pot odds (350 to call into a 900 pot, requiring about 28% equity) and the likelihood that the opponent is bluffing after checking back twice, a call is a profitable play.

AQo is a strong premium hand in a 3-handed game. Being on the button, I will open with a standard raise to 300, consistent with my previous opening sizes, to build the pot and take control of the hand in position.

Holding 8-4 offsuit is extremely weak and provides poor playability, especially from the small blind against a button raise. Calling would put me out of position with a dominated hand, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary at this stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I have an extremely weak, unsuited hand (4d 3s) out of position in the Big Blind. The Button (Flash) has opened to 3 big blinds. My hand has terrible playability, no high card strength, and no suitedness. Folding is the only reasonable decision.

Holding 3-9 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with very little equity, even in a three-handed game. As the button, I have no reason to enter this pot against the blinds, and folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

With a strong suited Ace (A9s) in the Small Blind after the Button folds, it's a clear open-raise opportunity. Raising to 3 BBs (300) applies pressure on the Big Blind to fold marginal hands, and we have excellent playability and equity if we get called.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Pro, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. While I have position on the Small Blind post-flop, Q2o is a very weak hand that does not hit many boards well and is easily dominated by Pro's raising range. Even with 2:1 pot odds, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K4o is a very weak unsuited hand. Even from the button, trying to steal the blinds with this holding is marginal and often leads to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the most profitable play in the long run.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c2c. Although the Button folded and it is only 50 more to call, 72 suited is one of the weakest hands in poker. Playing out of position against the Big Blind with such a weak hand is generally a losing strategy in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Kd Qd on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a very strong starting hand and a standard opening. Based on my previous play, a raise to 300 is consistent with my opening size and allows me to take control of the pot early while having position for the rest of the hand.

K9o is a weak hand to play out of position from the small blind against an aggressive button opener. Calling would force me to play post-flop out of position against two players, and 3-betting is not a strong move with this specific holding. Given the stack depths and my position, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

With pocket sixes in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x button raise from Flash, flat calling is the optimal play. We close the action and have deep enough stacks (effective ~8000) to profitably set-mine. If we flop a six, our implied odds are excellent. If we miss, we can easily check-fold to pressure without having committed too many chips.

On a dry, paired board like Jh 4c 4d, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play. Our pocket sixes are currently a marginal made hand (two pair, sixes and fours) that beats ace-highs and smaller pocket pairs, but loses to any jack, higher pocket pairs, or an unlikely four. By checking, we allow Flash to continuation bet with his bluffs and weaker hands, and we can re-evaluate our decision based on his sizing.

I hold Kd Qd on a Jh 4c 4d flop. Although I haven't paired yet, I have two overcards and a backdoor diamond draw. As the pre-flop aggressor on a dry, paired board, I have a significant range advantage. The Big Blind (Pro) checked to me, which is standard. I will make a continuation bet of 250 (roughly 38% of the pot) to take down the pot now or set up a turn barrel if I pick up equity. Pro has shown a tendency to fold to flop aggression in similar spots (Hand 7).

The opponent makes a small continuation bet of roughly 38% pot on a very dry, paired flop. Our pocket sixes are currently ahead of all his unpaired high cards (like AK, AQ, KQ) which will frequently c-bet this board. Raising here would only isolate us against stronger hands (like Jx, overpairs, or an unlikely 4x) while folding out his bluffs. Therefore, the most profitable decision is to call the small bet and evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings the Ten of Hearts, which is another overcard to our pocket sixes. This card connects heavily with Flash's pre-flop raising and flop c-betting range, hitting hands like AT, KT, QT, and adding equity to heart flush draws. Our hand is now a very marginal bluff-catcher. The standard play is to check to the aggressor to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on his turn action. If he fires a second barrel, we will likely have to fold.

On the turn, the Ten of Hearts (Th) significantly improves my equity as I now have a double gutshot straight draw (any Ace or Nine gives me a straight) along with my two overcards (King and Queen). Pro has checked to me again after calling my flop continuation bet. While a second barrel could potentially fold out pocket pairs (55-99) or weak Ace-high floats, Pro's call on the flop suggests they likely have a piece of the board, such as a Jack or a four. By checking back, I realize my equity for free, avoid a potential check-raise that would force me off my draws, and keep the pot manageable while still having the chance to hit a very strong hand on the river.

The river brings the Nine of Diamonds, putting a third overcard to our pocket sixes on the board. Flash checked behind on the turn, indicating a range likely capped at marginal pairs (which beat us) or missed draws like Ace-highs or flush draws (which we beat). Betting our pocket sixes here accomplishes nothing, as worse hands will fold and better hands will call or raise. Checking to see a free showdown is the optimal line. If Flash bets, our hand is too weak to bluff-catch given the coordinated runout, and we will fold.

I have completed a King-high straight (K-Q-J-T-9) on the river. The board is paired (4c 4d), which introduces the remote possibility of a full house, but Pro's line of checking the river after I checked back the turn suggests they are likely holding a Jack or a medium pocket pair and are in check-call mode. I will bet 750 for value, which is approximately 65% of the pot, to extract maximum value from a Jack or any other pair Pro might be unwilling to fold.

The river brings a third overcard to our pocket sixes, making the board incredibly dangerous. Against a Button raise to 2.5x, calling often leads to being dominated or facing difficult post-flop spots, and 3-betting with it as a bluff is less ideal than using suited connectors or suited Aces. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 4d, a suited one-gapper. Flashlight opened from the Button to 250. Pro folded the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more into a total pot of 400, giving me pot odds of 3.6-to-1. With these odds and the playability of suited connectors/gappers in a heads-up pot against the Button's wide opening range, a defense is standard. I will call and see a flop.

I have flopped trips (Three of a Kind, Fours) with a 6 kicker. On a board of 4s-4h-9c, this is an extremely strong hand. As the player in the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (Flashlight) to allow them to continuation bet with their wide range of overcards or pocket pairs. Leading out here often scares away hands that I want to keep in the pot. I will check and likely call or raise depending on the sizing of any bet from Flashlight.

The board is relatively dry (4s-4h-9c), and I hold two overcards (Ace-Ten). As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard to maintain the initiative and potentially take down the pot if the opponent has missed or is holding weak range cards. A bet of 300 represents roughly half the pot, which should be sufficient to test the opponent's holding.

I have flopped trips (Fours) on a very dry board (4s-4h-9c). Flashlight has made a standard continuation bet of 300 into a 550 pot. Since the board is extremely uncoordinated and I hold a monster hand that blocks many of the hands he would call a raise with (like middle pairs), raising now would likely force him to fold his bluffs or overcards. By calling, I keep his entire range in the pot, allowing him to continue bluffing on the turn or potentially catch a pair that he will then value-bet or call a lead-out with. My 6 kicker is weak for trips, but in a heads-up pot against a Button open, it is highly likely my hand is currently the best. I will slow-play here and re-evaluate on the turn.

On this turn of 2s, my hand remains extremely strong with Three of a Kind (Fours). The 2s is a safe card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Since I am out of position and have been slow-playing to induce bluffs or keep Flashlight's wider range in the pot, I will check again. This allows the pre-flop and flop aggressor to continue his story with another bet if he has air or a marginal hand like a pair of nines or a middle pocket pair. Checking also protects my checking range for the future.

The board (4s-4h-9c-2s) is relatively dry, and my opponent checked to me on the turn. I have Ace-Ten high, which has some showdown value but is likely behind a made hand like a pair of nines or a four. Since my C-bet on the flop was called, continuing to barrel on the turn with just Ace-high is risky. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free and prevents me from bloating the pot with a hand that has limited strength. I will take the free card to the river and re-evaluate then.

On the river, the board is 4s-4h-9c-2s-As. I have Three of a Kind (Fours) with a 6 kicker. After Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) checked back the turn, his range is likely capped at marginal made hands like a pair of nines, middle pocket pairs, or perhaps an Ace-high that decided to take a free card. The Ace on the river is a significant card; it's very likely to have improved many of Flashlight's overcard hands (like AJ, AT, etc.) to top pair. If I check, he might check back with a 9 or a medium pair to get to showdown. By betting, I can extract value from those improved Aces and possibly from a 9 if he believes I am bluffing the scare card. A bet of around half the pot (550) is a standard value size that is likely to get called by any Ace and occasionally a 9.

I am facing a bet of 550 into a pot of 1100 on the river. The board is 4s-4h-9c-2s-As. With my hand Ad-Ts, I have rivered top pair. The betting sequence (check-check turn) makes it quite possible that my opponent is turning a missed draw into a bluff, or value betting a weaker pair like a Nine or a Deuce. While a flush is possible given the three spades on the board, my opponent's line (check-call flop, check turn) is more consistent with a marginal hand or a draw that missed, rather than a slow-played flush. Given the pot odds, calling is the most profitable play as I have sufficient showdown value to catch a bluff.

Holding 23o on the Button, I have one of the weakest possible starting hands. With 64 BBs, there is no need to attempt a blind steal with a hand that has virtually no equity or playability post-flop if called. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine suited, a very strong hand for 3-handed play. Since the Button has folded, I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take initiative against the Big Blind. This is my standard opening size which I have used consistently throughout the tournament.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. Qs-3s is a playable, suited hand in a blind vs. blind situation. Since I am already committed for 100 chips and the call is 200, I am getting decent pot odds. Calling is the standard, disciplined approach to defend my big blind and evaluate the flop.

I have Ace-high with no hearts on a very dangerous monotone flop (7h-Qh-Th). This board is likely to have connected with the Big Blind's calling range, and without a heart in my hand, I have very little equity or protection against a flush or a strong draw. Betting here into the Big Blind is risky because I would be forced to fold to a raise and have no clear plan for most turn cards. Checking allows me to see how the opponent reacts and potentially take a free card to improve or evaluate the strength of their hand.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Queen with a 3). The board is very wet with three hearts, offering potential flush and straight draws. Since the small blind checked to me in position, checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity without bloating the pot against a range that might check-raise me with a stronger queen or a better draw.

The turn card 2s is a total brick. On the flop (7h-Qh-Th), both I and the Big Blind (Flashlight) checked. This suggests Flashlight likely doesn't have a made flush or a strong hand like top pair, as those hands usually bet for value and protection on such a coordinated board. My Ace-high (Ac-9c) still has some showdown value, but it's not strong enough to bet for value. Betting as a bluff is risky because any heart in Flashlight's hand might call. Checking again allows me to see a free river and potentially win at showdown if the action remains passive, or evaluate if Flashlight decides to bet.

I have top pair with a Queen, but the board is extremely dangerous with three hearts present. My kicker is weak (3), and Flash's check on the turn could indicate either a trap or a desire to realize showdown value with a marginal hand. Checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control and reach a showdown affordably without turning my hand into a bluff or bloating the pot against a potential flush.

The river Ace gives me top pair with a Nine kicker. The action on the flop and turn was very passive, with both players checking through. This suggests that Flashlight is unlikely to have a flush, a straight, or even a strong made hand like top pair (Queens) or a set, as those hands would typically bet for value or protection on such a coordinated board. By betting on the river, I am targeting middle-pair hands like a Ten or a Queen that might call a single bet thinking I am bluffing the Ace or just taking a late stab at the pot. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard value size here.

Flash checked through the flop and turn, indicating weakness and a lack of a strong Ace or flush. His river bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a relatively small sizing, which often represents a blocking bet or a weak value bet with an Ace. Given that I have a pair of Queens, I have significant showdown value against his likely bluffs or weaker pairs. Calling is the correct play given the pot odds and the weakness of his line, as I only need to be correct 25% of the time to break even.

I am on the button with 4c 2c. While it's a suited hand, it is extremely weak. I have been playing quite actively and the players behind me, especially Flashlight, have been calling and raising frequently. With a stack of 13530, I don't need to take unnecessary risks with such a low-equity hand from the button when a fold is the standard and safest play. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

With the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. While King-five offsuit is not a premium holding, it is a perfectly reasonable hand to complete the small blind with to see a cheap flop and evaluate the board texture.

Flashlight completed from the small blind. I'm in the big blind with Q4 suited, which is a weak hand but has some playability post-flop due to being suited. There is no need to bloat the pot pre-flop with an iso-raise given the hand strength. Checking allows me to see a flop for free in position.

The flop of Ace-King-Six of hearts is extremely wet and hits an opponent's big blind range very hard. With only a pair of Kings and a poor kicker, I am essentially playing a bluff catcher. Betting here would likely only be called by an Ace or a superior King, or potentially fold out hands I am currently beating. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how the opponent reacts on this dangerous board.

I have flopped the absolute nut flush, as the Ace and King of Hearts are on the board and I hold the Queen of Hearts. Flashlight has checked to me. I want to start building the pot to extract maximum value from hands like a single high heart (Jack or Ten), an Ace, or two pair. A half-pot bet of 100 is appropriately sized to keep his marginal hands in the pot while beginning to inflate it.

The board is quite wet with three hearts, and the opponent has led out with a small bet. My pair of Kings provides some showdown value, and holding the King of Clubs gives me a blocker to the flush. Given the small bet size relative to the pot, folding would be too tight and raising would likely isolate me against better hands or strong draws. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see a turn card to re-evaluate.

I hold a pair of Kings with a weak 5-kicker on a board of A-K-6-3. Since there is no bet to call, checking is the most appropriate action. The board is wet with three hearts, and betting into my opponent would likely isolate me against better hands like an Ace, a better King, or a flush. I should check to keep the pot manageable and see the river card.

I hold the absolute nut flush on this board, as the Ace and King of Hearts are on the table and I have the Queen of Hearts. Flashlight called my flop bet, indicating he likely has an Ace, King, or a single heart (like the Jack or Ten) that is now drawing dead. The turn card is a total blank. I want to continue building the pot and extracting maximum value from his made hands or dead draws. A bet of 300 will inflate the pot for a larger bet on the river while still keeping his calling range wide.

My pair of Kings has reasonable showdown value, and despite the wet board texture with three hearts, the pot odds (300 to call into a pot of 1000) are favorable. Pro's check pre-flop suggests their range might not be heavily weighted toward premium Aces, though they could definitely hold an Ace or a heart draw. Folding would be too conservative, and raising would likely isolate me against stronger hands. Calling allows me to reach the river to see if the board stabilizes or if I can check-fold if the action becomes too heavy.

The board texture is extremely dangerous with four hearts present, and any Ace or flush beats my pair of Kings. My hand has very little showdown value, and betting would be futile as I would only get called by better hands. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot and potentially reach a cheap showdown, though I must be prepared to fold to any significant bet from my opponent.

The river brings a fourth heart to the board. I hold the Queen of Hearts, and with the Ace and King of Hearts already on the board, I have the absolute nut flush. Flashlight checked to me again. Given the four hearts on the board, Flashlight is only calling a bet if he holds a heart of his own, such as the Jack or Ten of hearts. If he has a decent heart, he will call a large bet; if he has no heart, he will fold to any bet. Therefore, I should size my bet relatively large to extract maximum value from his second-best flushes. A bet of 800 (80% of the pot) is perfect for this.

The board has four hearts, creating a flush possibility for any player holding a heart. My pair of Kings with no heart is extremely vulnerable, losing to any Ace, any flush, or even various two-pair combinations. Given the opponent's aggressive betting pattern on the flop, turn, and river, it is highly probable that they have a made hand that beats my King. Calling here would be a mistake, as I am effectively bluff-catching against a range that is likely value-betting. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

Queen-Four offsuit is a very weak starting hand in a three-handed game. Even from the button, this hand has poor playability and is not a strong enough candidate to open-raise as a steal attempt against two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and standard strategy here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with A7 suited (A7s). The Button has folded, so it's a Blind versus Blind situation. A7s is a very strong hand to open with in this spot, both for value and to potentially steal the Big Blind. A raise to 3x the big blind (300) is standard to deny equity and build a pot with a hand that plays well post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Pro, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. Although I would have position post-flop, the hand strength of 4-3 offsuit is insufficient to continue even with 3:1 pot odds. Fold is the most disciplined play to maintain my chip lead.

I have Ace-King suited (Ks As) on the Button, which is one of the strongest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. I want to raise to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially extract value from worse Aces, Kings, or smaller pocket pairs. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my opening sizes.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o, which is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard 3x raise. While Button opening ranges are typically wide, Q7o lacks the equity and post-flop playability to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff from the worst position at the table. Given my current chip lead, I prefer to fold and wait for higher-equity opportunities rather than defending a marginal hand out of position.

My hand, 3d 8h, is extremely weak and disconnected, offering very little potential to improve or win the hand in a meaningful way. While I am in the big blind and getting decent pot odds to call, playing a weak hand like this out of position against a button raise is generally negative expected value (EV) in the long run. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. It is well ahead of the range of hands the blinds are likely to hold. I will put in a standard opening raise of 300 to build the pot and exert positional pressure on my opponents.

I am in the small blind with 4s 6s, a weak suited connector. Flash (the button) has opened to 300. Calling 250 out of position against a button raiser with this hand is generally negative expected value, as I will struggle to realize my equity post-flop, especially since I will be acting first throughout the hand. Given that there is still a big blind to act behind me, and my hand does not have enough strength to warrant a 3-bet or a call, the most disciplined and strategic action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

K5o is a weak, disconnected, offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button while being out of position in the Big Blind, calling or 3-betting is unprofitable over the long run. The most standard and disciplined action is to fold.

I am on the button with suited connectors (8c 9c), which is a strong hand to play in position. The action is folded to me. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to apply pressure on the blinds, build the pot, and potentially take control of the hand, which aligns with my previous aggressive play.

With King-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind facing a Button raise, I have a hand that is well ahead of the Button's wide opening range. 3-betting here is a strong play to either take down the pot pre-flop, avoid playing out of position in a multi-way pot if the Big Blind calls, or isolate the Button with the betting lead.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened from the Button and Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind. This situation indicates strong ranges from my opponents, and calling a 3-bet out of position with such a weak hand would be a significant mistake. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am holding 8c 9c, a strong suited connector, and I am in position as the initial raiser. Pro's 3-bet to 900 is a standard sizing. I have sufficient pot odds to call and see a flop, where I can leverage my position to navigate post-flop play. Folding would be too weak here, and 4-betting might inflate the pot unnecessarily with a drawing hand.

On a monotone Js Ks 2s flop, I have top pair with an excellent kicker and the second nut flush draw (Qs). Because the board is monotone, I want to use a smaller continuation bet size to get value from worse pairs, straight draws, or lower lone spades, while controlling the size of the pot in case my opponent already flopped a flush or holds the Ace of spades. A bet of around 1/3 pot achieves this.

I have 8c 9c with no pair and no spade on a board of Js Ks 2s. The board is extremely wet and favors ranges containing high spades or sets. With no equity (no flush draw, no straight draw, and only high-card potential), calling a bet on the flop into a three-way pot is mathematically incorrect. The opponent's bet signifies strength on a coordinated board, and I have nothing to continue with.
Q5o is a very weak offsuit hand. Even on the button, it is not worth opening, especially against two blinds who could easily wake up with better hands. Folding is the standard and optimal play here.

With the Button folding, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation. K7o is significantly stronger than the average hand in a head-to-head scenario against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately, or to define the hand if Flashlight calls.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h-2d, which is a bottom-tier starting hand. Even though I am getting decent pot odds to call the raise from the Small Blind, the hand has very poor playability and is dominated by almost any range Flash is raising with. It is best to fold and conserve my stack for a more profitable opportunity.

J9o is a standard opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. I'll stick to my previous sizing of 3x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position.

My hand, King of Clubs and Six of Spades, is weak and offsuit. Playing this out of position against a Button open raise is negative expected value and will likely lead to being dominated or forced into a difficult spot post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o facing a 3x open raise from the Button. My hand is unsuited, poorly connected, and has a very weak kicker. Playing this out of position is highly unprofitable due to reverse implied odds and poor playability. Folding is the correct decision.

I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit, which is a marginal but playable hand in a three-handed game. To maintain aggressive pressure and attempt to steal the blinds, a standard open raise is the appropriate move here. 300 is a consistent opening size based on the table's previous play.

While pocket deuces has some set-mining value, calling a 3x open from the small blind is marginal. We are out of position, the big blind can still squeeze, and we will miss our set 88% of the time, forcing us to check-fold. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here to preserve our stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Q5o. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 3x. Q5o is a weak hand to defend out of position; even against a wide button opening range, it plays poorly post-flop and rarely makes a strong enough hand to continue with confidence. Maintaining my significant chip lead is more important than defending a marginal hand in this spot, so I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.
J8o is a weak offsuit hand, and not strong enough to open from the Button even when trying to steal the blinds. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 2h 4s, which is a very weak hand. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind. While the cost to complete the bet is only 50 more chips, 2-4 offsuit has very little equity and I will be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand. It is more prudent to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am on the button with Q3o. While 3-handed play warrants a wider opening range, Q3o is significantly below the threshold for a profitable steal, especially since Flashlight and Pro have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. Given my chip lead, I can afford to wait for a better spot rather than committing chips with a hand that has poor post-flop playability.

With a low suited connector like 3d-4d in the small blind and the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. The pot offers decent odds (calling 50 to win a pot of 150), making it profitable to see a flop. Completing the bet is a standard and low-risk approach to keep the pot small and see the community cards.

Flashlight limped from the Small Blind. I have Q7o in the Big Blind, which is a weak, disconnected hand. It isn't strong enough to isolate with a raise, so taking a free flop in position is the best and standard play.

I am in the small blind with 3d-4d on a flop of 7s-Jh-2h. I have essentially no equity in this pot and have completely missed the board. Given that this is a limped pot, there is no pressure on me to bet. Checking is the most logical action to see if the opponent also checks or to surrender the pot if they show significant strength, as there is no value in bluffing with such a weak range on this texture.

I flopped middle pair with a Queen kicker on a J-7-2 board with a flush draw. Since the pot was limped pre-flop and Flashlight checked, a half-pot bet is a good play to protect my equity against overcards and charge any flush or straight draws. It also allows me to take the initiative in the hand.

I hold 3d-4d on a 7s-Jh-2h board. I have effectively missed the board completely and have no significant equity, not even a backdoor draw. With the opponent (Pro) betting 100 into a 200-chip pot, I have no reason to continue. This is an easy fold to preserve my stack.

I hold Ten-Deuce offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with very low equity, even in position. Despite being on the button, there is no strategic justification for calling 100 chips into a 150 chip pot with such a poor holding. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I have Jd Td in the small blind after the button folds. This is a strong suited connector that plays excellently post-flop. I will raise to 300 to attack the big blind, looking to either win the pot immediately or play a heads-up pot with the initiative and a highly playable hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven of hearts. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. With 100 already committed, it costs me 200 more to call into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 odds. My hand is suited and has decent playability, and I will have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. This is a standard defend against a Small Blind open.

I have flopped top pair with a very strong flush draw. This is a highly connected flop for my hand, giving me massive equity against almost any holding. I will continuation bet half-pot (300) to build the pot for value, charge worse flush draws or straight draws, and get value from worse pairs. I am more than happy to get chips in if raised.

I have flopped top pair with Jacks. While my kicker (7) is weak, I cannot fold to a single continuation bet on such a relatively dry board. Pro's lead for 300 into a 600 pot is a standard sizing that could represent a wide range of hands, including air, draws, or middle pairs. I will call to maintain pot control and utilize my position on future streets, allowing Pro to continue bluffing or betting with worse hands.

I have top pair with a good kicker, backed up by a strong diamond flush draw. The 8 of clubs on the turn introduces some new straight and club flush draws, but my top pair is likely still best against the big blind's wide floating range. To protect my hand from these draws and extract value from worse one-pair hands (like weaker Jacks, middle pocket pairs, or draws), I need to continue betting. A sizing of roughly two-thirds of the pot will effectively charge drawing hands while continuing to build the pot.

I have top pair with Jacks. While my kicker (7) is mediocre, folding here would be too tight in a blind-vs-blind situation on a board that hasn't changed significantly with the 8c. Pro's bet of 800 into 1200 (2/3 pot) shows strength, but it could also be a second barrel with a flush draw (diamonds) or a straight draw (like 67 or T9). By calling, I remain in position and can evaluate the river based on Pro's next move and whether the board becomes more dangerous.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a board where both diamond and club flush draws missed, but some straight draws (like 97, 43, or 74) have completed. Flash called substantial bets on both the flop and turn, indicating a range consisting of Jx, sticky middle pairs, or draws. If I bet the river, it is very thin for value; worse hands will likely fold, and I will primarily be called or raised by better hands like stronger Jacks, two pairs, or straights. Checking allows for pot control and gives Flash the opportunity to bluff with his missed flush draws.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (J7). After Pro double-barreled on the flop and turn and then checked the river, it's most likely he is either giving up on a bluff or has a marginal hand that he wants to take to showdown. There isn't much value in betting here, as better hands (like AJ, KJ, or QJ) will call or raise, and worse hands are unlikely to call a significant bet on this board. Checking back ensures I realize my equity and win the pot against missed draws and smaller pairs while avoiding a potential check-raise.
I have 95s on the button, which is a weak suited disconnected hand. Although we could attempt a steal, folding is the standard and safest play, preserving our stack for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 5h, which is a very weak hand. It's folded to me, so I'm only facing the Big Blind (Flashlight). While I could attempt a steal by raising, 3-5 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Flashlight has shown willingness to defend and play back. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with 7c 4h, which is a very weak hand. While I could attempt a steal, the players in the blinds have shown enough aggression in previous hands to make a 74o open unattractive. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

With Queen-high in the small blind and the button having folded, calling the additional 50 chips is a reasonable and standard play to see a flop cheaply in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I have position on the big blind post-flop, making this a justifiable call.

With King-Six offsuit in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind limp, I have a marginal holding. While I will have position post-flop, K6o is not strong enough to warrant an isolation raise. Checking to take a free flop and realizing my equity in position is the standard and most optimal play here.

I am in the small blind and checked/completed the blind pre-flop, meaning I am out of position against the big blind post-flop. The board of 5-9-10 with a club draw is coordinated and connects well with a big blind's defending range. With Queen-high and no pair or significant draw, I have very little equity to continue with if I face aggression. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and evaluate the big blind's reaction.

With King-high and a backdoor club draw on a coordinated 5-9-T flop, there is no need to bloat the pot. Flashlight's check indicates weakness, but my hand lacks the current strength or robust draws to justify a bet. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free and evaluate the turn.

With the pot being very small and neither player betting on the flop, there is no indication of strength from the big blind. My Queen-high has little showdown value, and the board texture (5-9-10-3) is reasonably coordinated, meaning the big blind could easily have a piece of it or a straight draw. Betting here with air is low-value and unnecessary, so checking is the most prudent way to minimize risk and see the river card for free.

Flashlight checks again on a relatively blank turn. My King-high still has some marginal showdown value, but I lack any meaningful draws to justify a semi-bluff. The board remains quite connected (lots of straight and flush draws available), meaning Flashlight could easily have a sticky draw or a weak pair that won't fold to a single bet. Checking behind is the safest play, allowing me to see a free river and potentially win at showdown if Flashlight is on a missed draw.

The river card 8d has made the board quite coordinated, completing potential straight draws like 6-7. My hand (Queen-high) has very little showdown value, and given that the pot is small and both players have checked through the flop and turn, it is highly likely that any call from my opponent will be a hand that beats mine. Checking is the most prudent play to minimize risk and avoid running into a better hand, while still potentially winning at showdown if the opponent is also holding air.

The river brings the 8d, and Flashlight checks for a third time. My King-high still retains some showdown value against busted club or diamond draws and random weaker high cards. In a small, limped pot where both players have shown no interest, trying to bluff at this board is unnecessary and risks getting called by a stubborn bottom pair or ace-high. Checking behind guarantees a free showdown and is the most optimal play to realize our remaining equity.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong hand in this position. Raising allows me to take control of the hand, potentially steal the blinds, or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300 to establish a standard open.
T5o is a very weak hand, and I am out of position in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. This is an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with J2 offsuit. Flashlight, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a standard late-position open. My hand is quite weak and plays poorly out of position. Given the stack sizes and the tournament progress, there is no need to defend such a marginal hand against a raise from the button, as I would be playing a bloated pot out of position with a weak range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

8h 4s is an extremely weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Even from the Button, it is not profitable to attempt a steal with absolute trash. The best decision is to fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 5h after the Button folded. This is a very weak hand. While SB vs BB play can be aggressive in 3-handed tournaments, 75o does not realize equity well out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a 3-handed game, I'm on the button and it's folded to me. Maintaining aggression from the button is key to picking up blinds and antes. While Q4o is a marginal hand, it's worth a standard 3x raise to 300 to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot in position. If I'm met with a 3-bet, the hand is weak enough to fold without much loss.
