I have AK-high on a board of 8-2-2-4. Pro called my flop continuation bet, which suggests he has a piece of the board (like an 8 or a small pocket pair) or a sticky Ace-high/draw. By checking back the turn, I control the pot size and realize my equity. Ace-high still has significant showdown value against Pro's possible floats, and checking avoids the risk of being check-raised off my equity. If an Ace or King falls on the river, I will be in a very strong position; otherwise, I can evaluate the river based on Pro's final action.

The Jh on the river connects with some of Flash's overcards that might have c-bet the flop and checked the turn (such as AJ, KJ, QJ, or JT). My pocket sixes still hold some showdown value against his missed broadways and Ace-high hands, but betting here has no value. A bet will likely only be called by a better hand like an 8, a Jack, or a higher pocket pair, while folding out the hands I currently beat. Checking allows me to get to a relatively cheap showdown or evaluate a potential bluff if Flash decides to bet.

On this board of 8-2-2-4-J, my Ace-King has significant showdown value. Pro called my flop continuation bet but has checked through both the turn and the river. While the Jack on the river is a potential scare card, it's more likely that Pro has a small pocket pair, a weak 8, or a missed float. By checking back, I realize my equity and win against all of Pro's missed draws and weaker Ace-high hands. Betting is unlikely to fold out a pair given the pot-control line I took on the turn, and checking ensures I don't lose more chips to a hand that currently beats me.

I am holding King-Three offsuit (K3o), which is a very weak hand in almost all positions. Even though I am on the button, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack.

With T5s in the small blind after the button folds, folding is the standard and most profitable play. The hand is too weak to limp or raise, and playing out of position against the big blind with a marginal hand will often lead to difficult spots.

KJo is a strong starting hand when first to act on the Button in a 3-handed game. I'll open with a standard raise to 3x the big blind to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position. KJo plays well in position, but I'll be careful if I face a 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h4h, a speculative suited connector. The Button (Pro) has opened for 300 (3x). While suited connectors have some playability, 6-high is very weak, and I will be playing the rest of the hand out of position against a competent and aggressive opponent. In a three-way dynamic, especially with roughly 36 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to fold these marginal holdings and wait for better opportunities rather than bleeding chips by calling out of position with weak cards.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Jack suited, a strong hand that plays well post-flop. Pro has opened from the button, and the price to call is favorable (200 to win a pot of 450). Given the stack depth and the strength of the cards, calling is the most standard and profitable play to see a flop.
I have top pair with a good kicker on a very dry board. Since I am out of position against the button raiser, checking is the standard play to gauge the opponent's strength and control the pot size.

The flop is quite dry with a paired deuce and a single queen, which strongly favors my pre-flop raising range over the Big Blind's calling range. By making a standard continuation bet of roughly a third of the pot, I can likely fold out a large portion of Flashlight's unpaired hands and smaller pocket pairs.

I have top pair (Queens) on a paired board with very little danger of draws. Pro's bet of 250 into a 900 pot is a standard continuation bet. Calling is the best play here to keep Pro's bluffs in the hand, maintain pot control, and see how the turn develops, as I am currently beating a significant portion of their range.

The board is 2d-2c-Qh-3h. I have top pair (Queens) on a paired board, which is a strong position but vulnerable to trips or a better queen. The 3h on the turn is relatively benign but adds straight draw potential for 5-4 or A-4. As I am out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the most sound strategy to control the pot size and induce bluffs or allow them to check back if they have a weaker hand.

My opponent called my continuation bet on the flop, suggesting they have connected with the board, holding perhaps a Queen, a medium pocket pair, or a flush draw. The 3 of hearts on the turn does not improve my King-high hand and brings a potential flush draw. Without strong equity or a solid semi-bluffing draw, firing a second barrel is unlikely to fold out better hands. Checking behind controls the pot size and allows me to see a free river card.

I have a full house (Queens full of Deuces) on a board with three deuces. Pro checked the turn and the river, suggesting a weak hand or a missed draw. Given the board texture, it is highly unlikely Pro holds a deuce, as they would likely have bet for value. I will place a value bet to extract chips from any weaker hands like Aces or a lower Queen.

The river brings a third deuce, meaning any pocket pair, Queen, or Three in my opponent's hand now makes a full house. Any Ace also outkicks my King-high. My hand is only Three of a Kind with King-Queen kickers, which loses to almost all of Flashlight's value range, including Ace-highs that decided to value bet. Since I only beat total air that completely missed the board, folding to the river bet is the only sensible play.

I am on the button with pocket sevens (7h 7s). In a 3-handed game, this is a very strong starting hand. I want to raise to build the pot and take control of the betting lead. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and puts pressure on the blinds.

Jack-three offsuit is a very weak hand, especially out of position in the small blind against a button open. Calling would put me in a tough spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff is not recommended with such a poor holding. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I hold Pocket Kings (KK) in the Big Blind facing a Button open from Flash. This is a premium hand, and a 3-bet is mandatory for value and to take control of the hand. Since I will be playing out of position post-flop, a larger 3-bet sizing of around 3.5x to 4x the open is optimal to reduce positional disadvantage and build the pot. I will 3-bet to 1100.

I hold pocket sevens (7h 7s) on the button. I opened to 300, and 'Pro' in the Big Blind 3-bet to 1100. Pocket sevens are very strong in a 3-handed game, but facing a large 3-bet from the chip leader, I have to consider the range. Pro's 3-bet size is substantial. While I could 4-bet, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that plays well in single-raised or 3-bet pots and maintains some pot control. I am in position for the rest of the hand, which is a major advantage. Calling 800 more to win a pot that will be 2250 provides reasonable odds to set-mine or play the post-flop aggressively on low boards.

With Pocket Kings on a very dry Q-2-3 rainbow flop, I have a massive range and nut advantage. Flash's pre-flop calling range to my 3-bet includes many hands I dominate, such as AQ, KQ, QJ, JJ, TT, and middle pocket pairs. Betting a smaller size (around 1/3 pot) allows me to extract value from these weaker hands while keeping his continuing range wide. It also provides some protection against random overcards like an Ace.

I am holding pocket sevens on a dry Queen-high flop (Qs, 2d, 3c). Pro lead out for 750 into a 2250 pot, which is a 1/3 pot continuation bet. Given the 3-handed nature of the game and the pre-flop 3-bet, this board hits a significant portion of a 3-betting range, but it is also a board where many hands like AK, AJ, or smaller pocket pairs will continuation bet. Folding here would be too tight given the pot odds (4:1) and the fact that sevens are still ahead of many non-Queen hands. Raising would likely only get called by better hands. I will call to see how the turn develops while utilizing my positional advantage.

The turn is the Ten of Diamonds, which introduces a flush draw and some straight draws, and improves hands like Pocket Tens or Queen-Ten. However, my Pocket Kings remain well ahead of the vast majority of Flash's flop calling range, which consists heavily of top pair hands (AQ, KQ, QJ) and worse pocket pairs (JJ, 99, 88). I need to continue betting for value and to charge any newly acquired draws. A bet of around half the pot sets up a reasonable river sizing and extracts value from one-pair hands.

Pro's line of 3-betting pre-flop and then double-barreling on a board that contains a Queen and a Ten is very strong. My pocket sevens are now significantly behind a range that includes AA, KK, QQ, JJ, TT, AQ, and KQ. The Ten on the turn is a particularly bad card for me as it improves JJ and TT. While I could be ahead of AK or AJ, Pro's sizing suggests they are committed to the hand. With a healthy stack of 7,580 chips, it is not worth risking a large portion of my stack on a marginal bluff-catcher in a 3-bet pot. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Ten-eight offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, and there is no reason to open the pot in this position. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice to conserve my stack.

With A2o in the Small Blind and the action folded to me, this is a prime opportunity to steal the blinds. Raising to 3x the big blind puts pressure on the Big Blind to defend with a wide range, and Ax is often ahead of their random holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 3h. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. This hand is weak and does not play well against a raise from Pro, who has a large stack and has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack.

Ten-Eight offsuit is a marginal-to-weak hand. Even from the Button, it is generally not profitable to open-raise this combination unless the blinds are extremely tight and passive. With a comfortable chip lead and no need to force the action with suboptimal holdings, folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd 9c. The Button (Pro) has folded. The pot is currently 150 (my 50 and Flashlight's 100). J9o is a reasonable hand to play heads-up against the Big Blind. Flashlight has been relatively active, but I can either call to see a flop cheaply or raise to try and take the blinds down immediately. Given the stack depths and the tournament stage, a raise is a standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind, but since the goal is maximizing the stack and Pro has folded, I'll put in a standard raise to 300 to exert positional pressure and define the hand.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong holding for defending the Big Blind against a Small Blind raise. With favorable pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400) and the positional advantage post-flop, calling is the most balanced and strategic approach to continue with this hand.

I raised pre-flop with J9o and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes 10-high and is quite dry (2c 5d Th). While I haven't paired up, this is a good board to continuation bet as the pre-flop aggressor. Most of the Big Blind's range consists of overcards that missed this flop, and a bet of around 40-50% of the pot should be enough to force folds from those hands. If I get called or raised, I will likely look to give up the hand unless the turn brings significant improvement.
With top pair (tens) on a dry board, calling is the most profitable play. It keeps the opponent's bluffs in the hand and controls the pot size while allowing me to reassess on the turn.

The turn brings the King of Diamonds, which is an excellent card for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Since I bet the flop and was called, continuing with a second barrel on this scare card is a strong play. Flashlight's range is likely capped at 10x or middle pairs, both of which will find it very difficult to continue against another bet on a board now featuring a King and a Ten. I am betting about 60% of the pot to maximize fold equity while representing a strong hand like AK, KQ, or even a set that slowed down on the flop.

Flash is betting on the turn after a King falls. While the King potentially pairs his range or gives him a better hand, my tens are still top pair and I am getting decent pot odds (roughly 3:1). Folding here is too weak, and raising is unnecessary given the board texture. Calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and re-evaluate on the river, while potentially inducing bluffs if Flash is continuing his aggression with a weaker hand.

The river is a 5, which pairs the board. I have Jack-high and no showdown value. I have triple-barreled in similar situations before, but Flashlight has called two streets on a board that should have been quite favorable to my perceived range (specifically the King on the turn). Their willingness to call a 650-chip bet on the turn suggests they likely have at least a Ten or a King, or possibly a five. Given Flashlight's previous play where they have shown a tendency to call down, a third barrel on the river is high-risk with a low probability of success. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown, though I expect to lose this pot to any pair.

I have a pair of Tens (T with an A kicker) on a board of 2-5-T-K-5. The King on the turn and the paired fives make this a difficult board for my Tens. Flash showed significant aggression by betting both the flop and the turn. If Flash is checking the river, it is either because he has a weak hand he is giving up on, or he is check-trapping with a King or a better hand. Given that any King or 5 beats me, and calling would be the only reason to stay in, checking behind is the safest play to showdown my hand without risking more chips on a potential value-cut situation.

KJh is a strong premium hand for a 3-handed button opening range. I'll raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size, to take control of the pot and build value against wider defending ranges from the blinds.

I have a strong starting hand with Ace-Queen offsuit in the small blind. Flash has opened from the button, and I want to take the initiative to either take down the pot pre-flop or isolate against them. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard, strong move to apply pressure and build the pot with a premium hand.

Flash opens from the Button, and Flashlight 3-bets from the Small Blind, indicating a strong range. My K7 suited in the Big Blind is far too weak to call a 3-bet out of position or to attempt a 4-bet bluff. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I opened to 300 with KJs, a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. Flashlight responded with a 3-bet to 900. While KJs is strong, calling the 3-bet in position is generally better than 4-betting here, as it allows me to keep Flashlight's bluffs in and use my positional advantage post-flop. 4-betting might force out hands I dominate and potentially get me into a massive pot against the top of their range. I will call the 600 more.

I have top pair with a strong kicker on an A-T-3 rainbow board. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the initiative. The board is relatively dry, so a continuation bet is standard to extract value from weaker aces, tens, or pocket pairs that may have called pre-flop. I will bet 600 into the 1900 pot to maintain control.

On this Ace-high board, my King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) is in a difficult spot against Flashlight's small blind 3-betting range. Flashlight's range heavily features strong Aces like AK, AQ, and AJ, which have me crushed. Although the bet size of 600 into a 1900 pot (offering roughly 4:1 odds) is relatively small, I only have 4 clean outs to the nuts. Calling here would likely lead to me having to fold on the turn if I don't improve, as Flashlight is likely to continue their aggression. Given that I am in a 3-bet pot and the board texture perfectly favors the 3-better's range, the most prudent play is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am holding a weak, unsuited hand (Jh-7d) on the button. While playing on the button offers position, the hand strength is insufficient to warrant an open-raise or a call against the blinds in this 3-handed scenario. Given the goal of maximizing the stack over 150 hands and the current state, a disciplined fold is the most strategic approach to conserve chips and wait for a more favorable hand.

32o is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With no positional advantage against the big blind and such weak cards, the standard and most optimal play is simply to fold. Trying to steal here is unnecessary given our stack size and the extremely poor hand equity.

Pocket Tens is a strong starting hand, especially in 3-handed play. Opening with a standard 3x raise from the button will build the pot for value, thin the field, and give me the initiative going into the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 3x the big blind. King-Deuce is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a large stack like Pro. Although it is a 3-handed game, this hand is at the bottom of my range and calling would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations without the lead or position. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s6c against a button raise. 56 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position. While the pot odds are decent to make the call, the long-term profitability of playing such a poor hand against an aggressive opponent is low. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 8-6 offsuit. This is a weak starting hand. While it is tempting to steal the blinds from the button, my stack size (roughly 54 BBs) is the shortest at the table, and both opponents have shown a willingness to play back or defend aggressively. Raising with such a weak hand risks a 3-bet from the blinds or being forced to play a marginal hand post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I hold pocket queens, which is a premium starting hand. The button has already folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. Raising is the optimal strategy to build a pot with such a strong hand and maintain the betting initiative, rather than just calling and allowing the big blind to see a free flop.

J9o is a marginal offsuit hand. Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, the pot odds and position are slightly tempting, but J9o plays poorly post-flop and can often end up dominated by better Jacks or Nines. With a very comfortable stack of 13,750 chips, there is no need to defend weakly here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding 4h 2s is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. It is unsuited, disconnected, and low-ranked. Since there has been no betting action yet (only the blinds), there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such a poor hand, even from the button position. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with 94o, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Even though the action folded to me and it only costs 50 chips to call, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable in the long run. I will fold.
Q2o is a very weak starting hand with low equity, no suitedness, and poor playability post-flop. As the first to act on the Button, this is a clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 92 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Pro has folded on the Button. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, 92o performs very poorly post-flop, and I would be out of position against Flashlight (the Big Blind). Given that I am the shortest stack at the table and need to preserve chips for better spots, there is no reason to play this hand.

I am on the button with Q5o. While a queen-high hand has some potential, it's generally a weak hand to open with in a three-handed game when the blinds are capable of playing back. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage (Hand 124 of 150), I'll choose to fold this hand to maintain my stack for better opportunities.

With the button having folded, I am in the small blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Since I have already committed 50 chips as the small blind, calling the remaining 50 to see a flop against the big blind is a standard and low-risk play.

With Q2 suited in the Big Blind after the Small Blind limps, there's no need to inflate the pot. Checking allows me to see a free flop in position. Raising with such a weak kicker is unnecessary and risky, as I'd be building a pot with a marginal holding.

The board is 10c-7c-Jh. I have Qc-9d, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (I need an 8 to complete a 7-8-9-10-J straight) and a backdoor flush draw potential with the Queen of Clubs. Given that I am in the small blind and it is a small pot, checking is the most prudent strategy to see a turn card cheaply and re-evaluate my position.

With Queen-high and no significant draws on a wet, coordinated board (Tc 7c Jh), I have very little equity. Since Flashlight checked to me, there is no need to commit chips into a pot where many of the opponent's limping hands have connected. Checking behind is the safest and most standard play.

The board is 10c-7c-Jh-5h. I hold Qc-9d, giving me a gutshot straight draw to an eight. The pot is very small at 200 chips, and the opponent (Big Blind) has checked both the flop and the turn, indicating they likely have a marginal hand or are playing passively. Since I do not have a made hand and my gutshot is not strong enough to warrant a bluff, checking is the most prudent and low-variance play to see the river for free.

The turn card brings the 5 of Hearts, giving me a Queen-high flush draw. Since Flashlight has checked twice, they likely have a weak holding or missed the board. This is a perfect spot for a semi-bluff. Betting puts pressure on their marginal hands and gives me fold equity, while I still have a strong draw to improve on the river if called.

The opponent's bet of 100 into a 200 pot gives me 3:1 pot odds, which is a favorable price to chase my gutshot straight draw (needing an eight) and my overcard (Queen). While the turn bet suggests the opponent might have a pair or better, the bet sizing is small, and there is enough equity to warrant a call to see if I hit my straight or improve on the river.

I have flopped a King-high straight with my Queen-Nine holding (9-T-J-Q-K). While there are three clubs on the board, making a flush possible, I hold a very strong hand. I will value bet 200 into the 300 pot to extract value from pairs, two-pair hands, or lower straights that may choose to call.

I missed my flush draw and am left with only Queen-high on a wet board featuring three clubs and straight possibilities. Flashlight is leading out for 200, representing a made hand or a completed draw. With absolutely no showdown value and a high risk of being called if I try to bluff, folding is the only profitable play here.

I am in the button position with J6 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand and does not justify a raise or a call, even in a three-handed game. I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger hand to preserve my stack.

With A6 suited in the small blind and the button folding, I have a strong hand for a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the big blind, take control of the hand, and potentially win the pot outright or play with a range advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Sevens (7s7h). The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, 77 is a very strong hand. Pro has been active, and I have a healthy stack. Calling is an option to keep the pot small, but I want to protect my hand and define my opponent's range. A 3-bet here is standard to take control of the hand, though since I am out of position against Pro if the button were in, but here Pro is the Small Blind so I will actually have position for the rest of the hand. Having position with a medium pocket pair is a great spot to raise. I will raise to 900 to put pressure on and potentially take the pot down now or set up a post-flop play with positional advantage.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, A6 suited is too marginal to call out of position. While it has some playability and blocks strong Aces, calling will lead to difficult post-flop spots, and 4-betting as a bluff carries unnecessary risk given our deep stack. Folding is the safest and most disciplined choice to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

J9o is a marginal offsuit one-gapper. From the button in a 3-handed game, this hand can sometimes be opened as a steal, but it's generally on the weaker side of the unsuited broadway/connector spectrum. Folding is the safest and most standard play to avoid difficult post-flop spots.

I have been dealt a pocket pair (6h 6s) in the Small Blind. The Button (Pro) has folded. The action is now on me with only the Big Blind (Flashlight) remaining. Pocket sixes are a strong hand in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and define the Big Blind's range. A standard 3x raise is effective here to either win the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop situation where I likely have the best hand pre-flop.

I am in the big blind with 53 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Flash has raised to 300 from the small blind. Calling with such a marginal hand out of position is not optimal. It is better to fold and wait for a more favorable spot to defend my big blind or enter a pot.

In a three-handed game, 74s is a marginal opening candidate from the button. While it is often played in GTO strategies, the current stack dynamics influence my decision. I am the shortest stack at the table with approximately 58 big blinds, while the Big Blind is the chip leader and the Small Blind also significantly covers me. Both opponents have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. Given my recent active image, I will choose to fold this hand to avoid over-extending with a weak holding and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (8-2 offsuit). Flash (button) has folded. There is no incentive to call or raise with such a poor hand out of position against the big blind. Folding is the most disciplined and strategically sound decision to preserve my stack.
I am on the button with suited connectors (6s 7s). This is a strong position to open the pot and attempt to steal the blinds. A raise to 300 is standard for the table dynamics.

K7o is too weak of a hand to play from the small blind against a button raise, even if the button's opening range is wide. Playing out of position with a marginal holding will lead to difficult post-flop situations. The optimal choice is to fold and preserve chips.

I am in the big blind holding J8s. Flashlight has raised to 3x from the button. J8s is a strong enough hand to defend against a button opening range, especially getting a decent price to call (200 to win 450). I have a solid stack and want to see a flop. Raising is an option but J8s plays well post-flop and calling keeps the pot manageable while I have a medium strength hand.

On this flop of Qs9s5d, I have a gutshot straight draw (to a Ten) and a flush draw with my Jack of Spades and Eight of Spades. This is a very strong draw with significant equity against almost any part of the button's range. Being in the big blind, checking to the initial raiser is the standard play to see how they react. I intend to check-call or check-raise depending on the sizing of a potential bet from Flashlight, as my hand has high potential but is currently only high-card strength.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have flopped a flush draw on a 9s Qs 5d board. This is a standard spot to make a continuation bet, leveraging my position and the equity of my flush draw, and potentially taking down the pot immediately given my opponent's check.

I have a very powerful draw on this Qs9s5d flop, holding both a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (to a Ten). The pot is 1000 and Flashlight has bet 350. My hand has significant equity against their range, and calling is a very profitable play here. Raising is also an option to put pressure on their air, but since I am out of position and have a hand that can realize its equity well by seeing more cards, calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and potentially stack my opponent if I hit my draw on the turn.

On the turn, the Jack of Clubs gives me second pair (Jacks) to go along with my flush draw and gutshot straight draw. This is a very strong semi-bluffing or catching hand. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. It allows me to see how Flashlight reacts to the Jack, which is a card that hits my defending range well. I can comfortably check-call a bet to realize my equity or check-back to see a free river.

I have significant equity here with a flush draw (9 outs) and a straight draw (3 non-spade 8s), totaling 12 outs. My opponent checked the turn, signaling weakness. I want to continue to apply pressure as the pre-flop aggressor and potentially take the pot down now, or build the pot to realize my equity if I hit on the river.

On the turn, the Jack of Clubs has improved my hand significantly. I now have second pair (Jacks) to go along with my flush draw (Js 8s on 9s Qs 5d) and a gutshot straight draw (any Ten). The pot is 1350 before the bet, and Flashlight has bet 700. My hand is now a very strong combination of made hand value and high-equity draw. Calling is the best play here; it allows me to realize my equity and keep Flashlight's bluffs in while not over-committing against a potential Queen or better. I have enough chips to comfortably call and see if my draw completes or if my pair of Jacks is good on the river.

On the river, the King of Hearts is a concerning card. It is an overcard to my pair of Jacks, and the board is now highly coordinated, with any Ten making a straight (9-T-J-Q-K). My pair of Jacks has fallen significantly in relative value, now acting as fourth pair on a board where many of Flashlight's button-opening and double-barreling hands (like Kx, Qx, or Tx) have improved or already beat me. I have some showdown value against missed non-spade draws or small pocket pairs, but I cannot expect to be called by worse if I bet. Therefore, checking to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or to evaluate Flashlight's reaction is the most appropriate move.

I am in position with a missed flush and straight draw (7-high). My opponent has called bets on both the flop and turn, indicating they likely have at least a pair. Bluffing on the river into a caller who has shown resilience is high-risk and unlikely to be profitable, especially given that the King on the river could have helped their range. Checking behind is the most prudent play to avoid losing additional chips when I have no showdown value.

Queen-six suited is a marginal hand. While it can theoretically be opened from the button in a 3-max game, folding is a safe and standard option to avoid difficult post-flop spots and preserve our comfortable chip lead.

I have KTo in the small blind. The button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the big blind (Flashlight) to contend with. KTo is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, well ahead of an average big blind's defending range. Given that the big blind only needs to call 50 more, I should raise to build the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or maintain initiative heading into the flop. I will make a standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips).
